notes: Paddle out from Alviso Marina into South Bay's tidal sloughs, where Ridgway's rails nest in the pickleweed and the mudflats define the tempo of every outing. Tidal range runs 9-10 feet, so plan your launch around the tide chart and aim to push off about an hour before low. Free parking, a floating dock, and gates that close at sunset.
geocode_display: Alviso Marina, Alviso, San Jose, Santa Clara County, California, 95002, United States

notes: Stevens Creek Reservoir sits tucked in the Santa Cruz Mountain foothills and is open only to human-powered craft, so the water stays quiet and the wildlife stays close. Launch fee is $5 total ($3 launch, $2 inspection), payable online in advance. No power boats means flat, calm conditions nearly every visit.
geocode_display: Stevens Creek Reservoir, Santa Clara County, California, United States

notes: Coyote Lake is a wide, open reservoir in the Coyote Creek watershed with enough fetch to catch an afternoon breeze. Power boats share the water, so expect occasional wake near the ramp. Launch fee is $5 ($3 launch, $2 inspection); arrive early on warm weekends.
geocode_display: Coyote Lake County Park Boat Launch, 10840 Coyote Lake Road, Gilroy, CA 95020

notes: Lexington Reservoir rewards calm-morning visits with glassy reflections of the redwood-studded hills above Los Gatos. Power boats are banned, keeping conditions predictable and the experience serene. Launch fee is $5 ($3 launch, $2 inspection).
geocode_display: Lexington Reservoir County Park, 17770, Los Gatos, Santa Clara County, California, 95032, United States

notes: Vasona Lake sits inside a manicured county park in Los Gatos and does not allow outside watercraft. SUP and kayak rentals are available through the park concessionaire during summer months, making it a solid option if you don't want to haul your own board.
geocode_display: Vasona Lake County Park, 333, Shannon, Los Gatos, Santa Clara County, California, 95032, United States

notes: Shoreline Lake in Mountain View is a fully managed paddle experience with rentals, lessons, and a $7 daily launch fee per vessel (or $150 annually for regulars). Power boats are prohibited, the water is flat, and PFDs are required on the water. Wind can pick up mid-afternoon, so morning sessions are best.
geocode_display: Shoreline Lake, Mountain View, Santa Clara County, California, United States

notes: The Palo Alto Baylands spread across 2,000 acres of pickleweed marsh, and the float basin offers calm, traffic-free water ideal for building bay skills before venturing into open water. Northeast winds of 10-15 mph are typical by afternoon, so an early start pays off. Free parking, no launch fee.
geocode_display: Palo Alto Baylands Nature Preserve (manual)

notes: Calero County Park's reservoir sits in rolling South Bay hills with tree-lined banks that buffer the wind on most days. Power boats are allowed, so expect some wake during busy afternoons. Launch fee is $5 ($3 launch, $2 inspection).
geocode_display: Calero County Park (Closed Area), San Jose, Santa Clara County, California, United States

notes: Lake Clementine on the North Fork of the American River splits into two sections. Upper Lake is paddle-craft only with no motors, open seasonally April through October. Lower Lake has a proper boat ramp and boat-in campsites, open year-round. Parking lots fill before 11 AM in summer, so go early.
geocode_display: Lake Clementine, Placer County, California, United States

notes: Rollins Lake spreads across the Nevada County foothills with multiple coves and timber shoreline, with launch access at Orchard Springs, Greenhorn, and Long Ravine campgrounds. Day-use launch fees run around $22-26 depending on the ramp. SUP and kayak rentals are available through Sierra Pontoon Rentals at Long Ravine on summer weekends.
geocode_display: Rollins Lake Road, Shady Glen, Placer County, CA

notes: Sand Harbor is one of Lake Tahoe's most striking put-ins, with crystal-clear water over granite boulders and sweeping mountain views just south of Incline Village. Entry is by timed reservation from mid-April through mid-October ($10-15 per vehicle), with an additional launch fee of $20-25. Clearly Tahoe Rentals stages gear directly on site if you need it.
geocode_display: Sand Harbor Beach, Washoe County, Nevada

notes: Pinecrest Lake sits at 5,600 feet in Stanislaus National Forest, a compact 300-acre reservoir ringed by pines with a 20-mph boat speed limit and no personal watercraft allowed. The marina rents SUPs and kayaks if you'd rather travel light. Calm mornings and cool mountain air make it a reliable Sierra day trip.
geocode_display: Pinecrest Lake Resort, 421, Pinecrest Lake Road, Strawberry, Tuolumne County, California, 95364, United States

notes: Jenkinson Lake at Sly Park Recreation Area is an El Dorado Irrigation District gem with forested shores and calm water well-suited to SUPs. Day-use entry is $13 per vehicle ($15 on holidays), and Current Adventures runs SUP and kayak rentals on the water Friday through Sunday, Memorial Day through Labor Day.
geocode_display: Jenkinson Lake, Sly Park, El Dorado County, California, United States

notes: Fallen Leaf Lake is a glacially carved gem tucked just south of Lake Tahoe with the same signature blue water but far less boat traffic. Non-motorized launch fee is $5, and TRPA requires a current aquatic invasive species inspection before you put in. The marina rental shop is open Memorial Day through October 1.
geocode_display: Fallen Leaf Lake Store, 400, Fallen Leaf Road, Fallen Leaf, South Lake Tahoe, El Dorado County, California, 96150, United States

notes: Waterman's Landing in Carnelian Bay is North Lake Tahoe's most complete paddling hub: a dog-friendly beach, a cafe serving breakfast and lunch until 3 PM, and a full fleet of SUPs, kayaks, and outrigger canoes for rent. The water is Tahoe-clear and the setting faces west toward the Sierra crest. It's genuinely the most welcoming put-in on the north shore.
geocode_display: Lake Tahoe, Placer County, California, United States

notes: Aquatic Park Cove is sheltered from Bay chop by the long arm of Municipal Pier, making it the calmest open-water launch in San Francisco. The beach is free and public, and the protected cove is a natural starting point before venturing toward Ghirardelli Square or the piers. Water temperature stays in the low 50s-60s, so a wetsuit is strongly recommended.
geocode_display: Aquatic Cove, San Francisco, California, 94123, United States

notes: Crane Cove Park in the Dogpatch neighborhood is sheltered by the dry docks to the south and Pier 50 to the north, producing calmer water than almost anywhere else on the SF waterfront. Dogpatch Paddle operates nearby with rentals and guided trips; expect harbor seals, herons, and skyline views. Swimming is not allowed due to submerged industrial debris.
geocode_display: San Francisco, California, United States

notes: Launch from the ADA-accessible floating dock at Mission Creek Park (401 Berry Street) and you're 10-15 minutes from McCovey Cove by paddle, with Bay Bridge views the whole way. The creek itself is sheltered and nearly current-free. On Giants game days the cove gets crowded with ferries and spectator boats, so stay alert outside the creek channel.
geocode_display: Mission Creek Kayak Launch, 401 Berry St, San Francisco, CA 94158

notes: Black Miners Bar on Folsom Lake gives you immediate access to the upper reservoir arms where canyon walls keep the wind modest and the scenery dramatic. It's inside Folsom Lake State Recreation Area, so the $10 day-use entry covers your visit. Arrive early on summer weekends before the parking fills.
geocode_display: Folsome Lake Black Miners Bar State Park, Represa, Folsom, Sacramento County, California, United States

notes: Folsom Lake offers more launch variety than almost any reservoir in Northern California, with access points at Peninsula Campground, Rattlesnake Bar, Granite Beach, Browns Ravine, and Beeks Bite, each offering a different character of cove or open water. Afternoon wind can build quickly on the main lake. Check which ramps are open seasonally, as water levels fluctuate significantly.
geocode_display: Folsom Lake, Folsom, Placer County, California, United States

notes: Lake Natoma is the calmer, lower reservoir on the American River Parkway, with three distinct put-ins: Black Miners Bar, Willow Creek, and Natoma Flat. It's a favorite for consistent flatwater without the exposure of the main Folsom Lake arms. No launch fee, easy shoreline access, and a bike trail running the length of the lake for a great post-paddle stretch.
geocode_display: Lake Natoma, Folsom Junction, Folsom, Sacramento County, California, United States

notes: Foster City's interconnected lagoons offer calm, motor-free water with a 5-mph speed limit, making them one of the most beginner-friendly venues on the Peninsula. Launch from Leo J. Ryan Park or the Boat Park near the dog park at the south end. California Windsurfing rents boards and kayaks from the Boat Shed at Ryan Park.
geocode_display: Foster City, San Mateo County, California, United States

notes: Pillar Point Harbor in Half Moon Bay is one of the most accessible protected-water launches on the Peninsula coast, with extensive breakwaters keeping the harbor calm even when the open ocean is rough. Sea lions and harbor seals haul out near the yacht club, and Half Moon Bay Kayak Co. offers rentals and guided tours. Launch from the public side of the Half Moon Bay Yacht Club.
geocode_display: Pillar Point Harbor Beach, Half Moon Bay, San Mateo County, California, United States

notes: The Chesapeake Drive ramp at Redwood City Municipal Marina puts you on Redwood Creek with quick access to the Bay Water Trail and open bay. Large commercial ships use the main channel, so paddle inside the sloughs rather than lingering near the shipping lane. The ramp is free for kayaks and SUPs, with restrooms in the marina building.
geocode_display: Chesapeake Drive Boat Ramp, 601 Chesapeake Dr, Redwood City, CA 94063

notes: Redwood Creek and Bair Island's tidal channels wind through 3,000 acres of salt marsh where harbor seals pup in spring and hundreds of shorebird species stop on the Pacific Flyway. Stick to mid or high tide or you'll bottom out in the muck before reaching the inner sloughs. Wildlife density here is among the highest of any Bay paddle.
geocode_display: Redwood Creek, Redwood City, San Mateo County, California, United States

notes: Westpoint Harbor is a private marina in Redwood City, but the far end of the parking lot has a free public launch for SUPs and kayaks on the left side. 101 Surf Sports stages rentals on weekends if you need gear. From here you can reach open Bay or thread the nearby sloughs depending on your comfort level.
geocode_display: Westpoint Harbor, 101, Westpoint Harbor Drive, Pacific Shores Center, Redwood City, San Mateo County, California, 94063, United States

notes: Seal Point Park's shoreline in San Mateo sits on a former landfill peninsula with broad bay views and a dedicated kayak and windsurf launch adjacent to Ryder Park. The water is open South Bay with moderate tidal current, a solid intermediate option with easy parking and a paved path along the water's edge.
geocode_display: San Mateo County, California, United States

notes: Bodega Harbor's Westside Regional Park features a three-lane boat ramp and the first wheelchair-accessible kayak launch in Sonoma County. The harbor is well-protected from the open Pacific swell, manageable even when the coast is rough. Free launch, no day-use fee, open daily until sunset.
geocode_display: Bodega Bay, Sonoma County, California, 94923, United States

notes: Corte Madera Creek winds past Larkspur's houseboat community in a protected tidal environment that stays calm even when Marin ridgelines are channeling wind. Launch from Bon Air Landing Park or Piper Park; paddle right from the dock to explore upstream past the Bon Air Road bridge at high tide. It's tidal, so check the chart and paddle with the flow.
geocode_display: Corte Madera Creek, Larkspur, Marin County, California, United States

notes: Steelhead Beach Regional Park in Forestville gives you direct river access with a year-round beach launch. Summer flow runs 3-5 knots and the river is friendly for recreational boards; spring flows can hit 8-10 knots after any rain, so check conditions. Parking is $7 (free for Regional Parks members) and the lot fills fast on summer weekends.
geocode_display: Steelhead Beach, Forestville, Sonoma County

water: Russian River - Sunset Beach River Park
notes: Sunset Beach River Park, locally called Mom's Beach, is a wide sandy stretch backed by redwoods just a short walk from the parking area. It's a natural midpoint on the Steelhead-to-Sunset float, a popular 2-hour scenic stretch. Free loaner life vests are available on the beach in summer, and a regional shuttle runs weekends Memorial Day through Labor Day.
geocode_display: Sunset Beach River Park, Forestville, Sonoma County

notes: Lake Sonoma fills the arms of Dry Creek Valley behind Warm Springs Dam with 50 miles of shoreline and almost no visible development from the water. Launch free at Yorty Creek (car-top only) or pay $3-20 at the marina ramp near the dam. The western coves offer the most sheltered paddling; midlake wind can build by noon.
geocode_display: Lake Sonoma, Sonoma County, California, United States

notes: Johnson's Beach in Guerneville sits in the heart of the Russian River's most social stretch, where a seasonal dam in summer creates flat, lake-like conditions. Rentals are available on-site from Russian River Tubes and Kings Russian River from mid-June through late September. Weekday mornings are peaceful; summer weekends bring the crowds.
geocode_display: Guerneville, Sonoma County, California, United States

notes: Healdsburg Riverfront Regional Park lines the Russian River just outside downtown Healdsburg, with two oxbow lakes inside the park for quiet, no-motor paddling separate from the main river. Access to the lakes requires a short walk with your board. River's Edge Kayak and Canoe nearby offers guided trips and shuttle services for full river floats.
geocode_display: Healdsburg, Sonoma County, California, 95448, United States

notes: Healdsburg Veterans Memorial Beach is the social anchor of the local Russian River scene, with a broad sandy beach, lifeguards on duty in season, and easy flat-water access on a gentle Class I stretch. Paddling season runs May through September. Afternoon winds can funnel through the valley, so morning sessions are the smoothest.
geocode_display: Russian River Rose Co., 1685, Magnolia Drive, Healdsburg, Sonoma County, California, 95448, United States

notes: Heart's Desire Beach on Tomales Bay sits inside Tomales Bay State Park facing the protected inner bay, which keeps the water calm and the launch manageable. Blue Waters Kayaking stages rentals and SUP gear on-site on weekends in season. Afternoon winds pick up quickly, so aim for morning. Park entry is $7-8 per vehicle and the lot fills before noon on sunny days.
geocode_display: Heart's Desire Beach, Marin County, California, United States

notes: Lake Berryessa is Napa County's big flatwater option, a 25-mile reservoir with dramatic canyon topography and surprisingly little congestion outside peak summer holidays. Launch free at the Putah Creek car-top access or use the Capell Cove or Markley Cove ramps for a small fee. The lake is deep, clear, and exposed to afternoon valley wind, so commit to morning paddles for the best conditions.
geocode_display: Lake Berryessa, Napa County, California, United States

notes: Tomales Bay's narrow, protected waters run 15 miles along the San Andreas Fault, offering some of the calmest paddling in the North Bay. Launch from Miller Boat Launch on the east shore, which has a two-lane ramp and parking for $5. Watch for afternoon NW winds and opposing tides mid-bay near Hog Island, where chop can build quickly.
geocode_display: Tomales Bay, Cypress Grove, Marin County, California, 94940, United States

notes: The Mill Valley Bayfront Park sits at the edge of Pickleweed Inlet on Richardson Bay, with an ADA-accessible gangway and low-freeboard dock drop-in. The inlet offers calm tidal paddling through marsh channels rich with birdlife. The launch is unusable at low tide when mudflats extend well into the inlet, so checking tides before arriving is essential.
geocode_display: Bayfront Park, Alto, Mill Valley, Marin County, California, United States

notes: The Napa River through downtown Napa is a surprisingly mellow tidal paddle with winery-flanked banks and almost no boat traffic. Launch free from the Jim Hench Memorial Kayak Launch at Oxbow Commons or the Main Street Dock. Morning sessions are calmest; afternoon NW winds can push upriver and create a convenient downwind run back.
geocode_display: Napa River, Napa County, California, United States

notes: Novato Creek winds through tule marshes from its Stafford Lake Park access point, offering a quiet estuary experience close to town. The creek becomes increasingly tidal as you head toward San Pablo Bay. Plan around a mid-to-high tide to keep adequate water under your board.
geocode_display: Novato Creek, Novato, Marin County, California, United States

notes: The Petaluma Marina offers a 24-hour public boat ramp with easy highway access and ample parking, making it one of the most convenient river launches in the North Bay. The river is tidal and generally calm, ideal for mellow out-and-back sessions through marshland. Stick to mornings in spring, when afternoon NW winds can make the return a slog.
geocode_display: Petaluma River, Petaluma, Sonoma County, California, United States

notes: Drakes Estero is a pristine tidal estuary inside Point Reyes National Seashore with glassy water, harbor seals, and almost no powerboat traffic. The launch site is 0.7 miles down a dirt road off Sir Francis Drake Boulevard near the Schooner Creek bridge. Note the mandatory closure from March 1 through June 30 for harbor seal pupping season; outside that window, time your session around mid-to-high tide to avoid getting stranded on mudflats.
geocode_display: Drakes Estero, Marin County, California, 94937, United States

notes: China Camp State Park puts you on a scenic gravel beach with direct access to the protected waters of San Pablo Bay, framed by tidal marshes and rolling hills. Launch from the beach at China Camp Village at mid or high tide. At low tide the mud is deep and sticky, so check the tide chart before you load the car.
geocode_display: China Camp State Park, San Rafael, Marin County, California, United States

notes: Gallinas Creek flows out of San Rafael's wetlands into San Pablo Bay and is best accessed at Buck's Landing (665 N. San Pedro Road), a small Marin County park with free parking and a shore launch. The creek winds through protected marsh, making it a calm, wildlife-rich paddle. Current picks up near the bay mouth, so watch conditions on the outbound leg.
geocode_display: San Rafael, Marin County, California, United States

notes: McNear's Beach has a paved ramp right off the parking lot and sits on the Bay Water Trail, with easy access to open San Pablo Bay. Mudflats extend far offshore at low tide, making launch and landing genuinely difficult. Come at mid or high tide and watch for strong currents near the pier.
geocode_display: McNears Beach County Park, San Rafael, Marin County

notes: Nagasawa Community Park in Santa Rosa provides a quiet urban put-in on a small waterway connected to the broader Sonoma County creek system. Conditions are calm and sheltered, suitable for a relaxed out-and-back session. Water levels can run low in late summer, so check conditions before heading out.
geocode_display: Santa Rosa, Sonoma County, California, United States

notes: Spring Lake in Santa Rosa is a calm, motor-limited lake with a year-round paved ramp at the Newanga Avenue entrance. Rentals are available through Sonoma County Parks from Memorial Day through Labor Day. Life jackets are required on the water, and a parking fee applies.
geocode_display: Spring Lake Park, Melita, Santa Rosa, Sonoma County, California, United States

notes: Dunphy Park is the go-to launch for exploring Sausalito's waterfront and Richardson Bay, with a small ADA-accessible beach and free parking steps from the water. From here you can paddle east toward Angel Island (roughly 3 miles) or north along the Sausalito waterfront. Watch for afternoon westerly winds that can make the return push.
geocode_display: Dunphy Park, Sausalito, Marin County, California, United States

notes: Horseshoe Cove sits directly beneath the Marin tower of the Golden Gate Bridge at Fort Baker, giving you one of the most dramatic launch backdrops in the Bay. Free parking is right at the water's edge, and the cove is calm enough for an easy put-in. Currents at the Gate can reach 6 knots on a strong ebb, so check the tide tables before heading outside the cove.
geocode_display: Horseshoe Cove, Fort Baker, Sausalito (manual)

notes: Richardson Bay is a broad, shallow arm of the Bay between Sausalito and Tiburon, popular for its protected water and straightforward wind patterns. Launch from Dunphy Park or Schoonmaker Point for access to the full bay. Be aware that roughly 900 acres of the southern bay fall within an Audubon Sanctuary with seasonal boat closures for migratory birds.
geocode_display: Richardson Bay, Belvedere, Marin County, California, United States

notes: The Sausalito waterfront is best launched from Dunphy Park at high tide or from one of the marina boat ramps for a flatter entry. Wind can funnel hard through the Gate on spring and summer afternoons, so morning sessions are more predictable. The flat water between Sausalito and the Marin headlands rewards early risers with glassy conditions.
geocode_display: Sausalito, Marin County, California, 94965, United States

notes: The Russian River through Guerneville and Healdsburg offers lazy summer paddling through redwood-lined banks, with rental operations at Johnson's Beach and Veterans Memorial Beach. Conditions are best June through early October when the river runs low, clear, and calm. Ask local outfitters about current depth before committing to a long stretch in August.
geocode_display: Russian River, California, United States

notes: Blackie's Pasture in Tiburon has two free parking lots and a small beach opening onto Richardson Bay, with views across to San Francisco and Angel Island. A portion of the bay falls within the Audubon Sanctuary, which restricts watercraft during migratory bird season, so verify the boundary before launching. Calm mornings here are genuinely quiet and scenic.
geocode_display: Blackie's Pasture, Little Reed Heights, Tiburon, Marin County, California, United States

notes: Strawberry Point juts into Richardson Bay and gives you access to calm, sheltered water from a dock at the end of E. Strawberry Drive. The inner bay is beginner-friendly and well-sheltered from direct Gate wind. Note that the Audubon Sanctuary boundary covers sections of the southern bay, so stay aware of the no-go zone during closure season.
geocode_display: Tiburon Boulevard & East Strawberry Drive, Tiburon Boulevard, Bel Aire, Marin County, California, 94925, United States

notes: The Alameda Estuary is a narrow, sheltered channel running between Alameda and Oakland, with almost no swells and minimal powerboat traffic. Multiple street-end access points make it easy to drop in without a formal ramp. The flat, protected water is reliable year-round.
geocode_display: Oakland Marinas - Oakland Alameda Estuary, Embarcadero West, Jack London Square, Downtown Oakland, Oakland, Alameda County, California, 94607, United States

notes: Crown Beach (officially Robert Crown Memorial Beach) is a half-mile stretch of sandy East Bay shoreline with open bay access and a gentle beach launch. At low or minus tide the beach flattens out dramatically and launching becomes very difficult. Plan around mid to high tide. Paid parking in the lot (reasonable rates). Wind and chop pick up in the afternoon, so early sessions are smoother. Busy on summer weekends.
geocode_display: Robert W. Crown Memorial State Beach, Alameda, Alameda County, California, United States

notes: Encinal Beach is a quiet Alameda shoreline with direct access to the Bay, sheltered enough for relaxed paddling on calm days. Like Crown Beach nearby, conditions are poor at low or minus tide: the beach exposes and launch is impractical. Aim for mid to high tide. The beach launch is informal and free, with street parking nearby.
geocode_display: Encinal beach, Alameda, Alameda County, California, United States

water: Encinal Boat Ramp to Ballena Bay
notes: The Encinal Boat Ramp gives you a clean, paved launch into the protected inner estuary, with the option to paddle north along Crown Beach or southeast into Ballena Bay's calm marina waters. The route covers some of Alameda's most scenic waterfront and stays well-sheltered from the open Bay. The ramp is free and accessible at all tide levels.
geocode_display: Encinal Boat Ramp, Alameda County

notes: Albany Beach is a wide sandy shore tucked behind the Albany Bulb, offering a semi-sheltered basin that softens Bay chop on calmer days. The beach launch is free, with ADA parking and restrooms on site. Avoid the Albany Mudflats Ecological Reserve to the north, which is a state marine park closed to watercraft.
geocode_display: Albany Beach, Albany, Alameda County, California, United States

notes: Big Break Regional Shoreline in Oakley is the gateway to the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta, with a free beach launch about a quarter mile from the parking lot. Wheeled kayak carts are loaned free from the Visitor Center. The calm, island-dotted Delta channels here are some of the most unique paddling in the Bay Area.
geocode_display: Big Break Regional Shoreline, 69 Big Break Rd, Oakley, CA 94561

notes: Berkeley Marina has a protected inner basin shielded from open Bay wind, with multiple access points and a long pier that acts as a natural windbreak. Launch from the marina's public beach for a straightforward flat-water session. More ambitious routes from here include a crossing to Angel Island or paddling the Berkeley shoreline south toward Emeryville.
geocode_display: Berkeley Marina, Berkeley, Alameda County, California, 94710, United States

notes: Lake Del Valle is a seven-mile reservoir in the Livermore hills with a 10 mph speed limit for all vessels, keeping the water unusually calm for a lake this size. Launch from the East Beach ramp with a nominal launch and inspection fee on top of parking. SUP rentals are available seasonally through Outback Adventures, and summer water temperatures are warm enough to skip the wetsuit.
geocode_display: Del Valle Regional Park, Livermore, Alameda County, California, 94550, United States

notes: The Oakland Estuary at Jack London Square is a flat, protected urban channel with a free public dock at the Jack London Aquatic Center about half a mile east of the square. California Canoe and Kayak at 409 Water Street rents boards and kayaks if you don't have your own. The estuary stays calm regardless of Bay conditions, making it a reliable option when wind shuts down other spots.
geocode_display: Oakland, Alameda County, California, United States

notes: San Leandro Bay is a shallow, protected tidal basin with solid access at Tidewater in Oakland and the boat ramp at the end of Grand Avenue in Alameda. The MLK Jr. Regional Shoreline boat launch also opens up the airport channel. Stick to mid or high tide since the bay shallows quickly and mudflats can trap you if you linger too long.
geocode_display: San Leandro Bay, East Oakland, Oakland, Alameda County, California, United States

notes: Shadow Cliffs is a former gravel quarry turned swimming and paddling lake in Pleasanton, with electric-motor-only rules that keep the water smooth. Parking is $6, with a one-time $4 watercraft inspection fee and a $2 launch fee. The warm, calm water makes it one of the most beginner-friendly flatwater venues in the East Bay.
geocode_display: Shadow Cliffs Regional Recreation Area, 2500 Stanley Blvd, Pleasanton, CA 94566

notes: Keller Beach in Point Richmond is a wide sandy bay beach on the Bay Water Trail, with a steep quarter-mile path from roadside parking down to the water. Bring a wheeled dolly for your board. The beach is calm and scenic, but check water quality advisories before you go, especially within three days of rain.
geocode_display: Keller Beach, Richmond, Contra Costa County, California, United States

notes: Point Pinole Regional Shoreline offers open San Pablo Bay access with a quarter-mile walk from the parking area to the Pinole Point put-in. Low tides expose extensive mudflats, making launch and landing very difficult, so check the tide chart carefully. Stay at least 330 feet from harbor seal haul-outs on the shoreline.
geocode_display: Point Pinole Regional Shoreline, 5551, Richmond, Contra Costa County, California, 94806, United States

notes: Richmond Marina is a sheltered inner harbor with a reliable, straightforward launch for accessing San Pablo Bay. The calm marina basin is forgiving in most conditions, making it a solid fallback when wind makes more exposed spots unpleasant. Restrooms and parking are right at the water.
geocode_display: Marina Bay, Richmond, Contra Costa County, California, 94804, United States

notes: Lodi Lake Park is a calm oxbow lake with a free shore launch and direct connection to the Mokelumne River for those who want to extend the paddle. The river section from the fish hatchery is beginner-friendly with no significant rapids, though shallow sections emerge by late summer. The Lodi Boathouse runs seasonal rentals and guided river tours on weekends.
geocode_display: Lodi Lake, Lodi, San Joaquin County, California, United States

notes: Elkhorn Slough in Moss Landing is one of California's best wildlife paddling experiences, with sea otters, harbor seals, and shorebirds in nearly every channel. Launch from Kirby Park (free parking, floating dock, inland access off Elkhorn Road) or from Moss Landing Harbor, where outfitters rent boards on site. Tidal currents run strongest near the ocean mouth, so start your session on a flood tide to ride it inland.
geocode_display: Elkhorn Slough, Moss Landing, Monterey County, California, United States

notes: Loch Lomond Reservoir in San Rafael is a city-managed drinking water lake with a $10 vehicle fee. Outside watercraft are not permitted, so you must rent from the on-site fleet. It's a scenic, peaceful setting on the edge of the Marin hills.
geocode_display: Loch Lomond Reservoir, Lompico, Santa Cruz County, California, United States

notes: Three dedicated launch ramps serve windsurfers, kiteboarders, and paddleboarders along 149 acres of SF Bay shoreline. The 2022 Promenade renovation added a sheltered crenulate bay and sandy beach, giving SUPs a calmer inside option. Entry fee is $6 per vehicle; SUP rentals are available on site through Boardsports California.

A sheltered cove beach protected from prevailing winds, rated beginner-friendly for SUP and kayak launches from the gradual southern slope. Best at mid-to-high rising tide when the shore break stays small; on flat days it rivals any protected Bay launch. Local surf shops rent boards and kayaks year-round.

A public concrete launch ramp at Sierra Point gives direct access to the open SF Bay with city skyline views and a 300-foot fishing pier nearby. The Bay Water Trail runs adjacent, and the ramp serves human-powered craft alongside small trailered boats. Wind and chop build by midday, so early morning paddles are best.

A paved put-in inside Don Edwards San Francisco Bay National Wildlife Refuge, offering up to 10 miles of protected slough paddling through prime shorebird and waterfowl habitat. No fee and no inspection required; the calm tidal channels suit SUPs well when timed to the flood tide. An endangered saltmarsh harvest mouse lives in the adjacent vegetation, so stay on the water.

A 232-acre non-motorized reservoir tucked into the Santa Cruz Mountain foothills, open to kayaks, canoes, and paddleboards on calm, glassy water. All Santa Clara County vessels require the county quagga mussel inspection before launch. The reservoir can close during low-water years, so calling ahead is strongly advised.

A baylands access point off McCarthy Boulevard giving paddlers a free launch into the tidal salt marsh connecting to Don Edwards Refuge and Alviso Slough. Tidal velocity runs around 1.5 mph with a 9-to-10-foot range, so timing the paddle with the flood is critical. Some sections close March through August for heron nesting.

A calm 83-acre lake in Fremont's Central Park with a dedicated carry-down launch. Rentals including SUP boards, kayaks, and pedal boats are available on weekends and holidays from April through September. No motorized boats allowed, making conditions consistently smooth for beginners.

A serene 315-acre reservoir tucked into the Oakland Hills with kayak and SUP access via a carry-in launch for $3. All vessels must pass a mandatory quagga and zebra mussel inspection ($4 fee) before entering. Rentals are available at the marina, and the surrounding 20-mile trail network adds strong value to the visit.

A rare urban paddle in the heart of Oakland on a tidal lagoon ringed by parks and grand architecture. The public non-motorized launch at 568 Bellevue Avenue is free, with sailboat, kayak, and SUP rentals available seasonally. Lake Merritt is a California Historic Landmark and one of the most scenic flatwater sessions in the East Bay.

Former sand and gravel quarries converted into a chain of clear freshwater lakes with a roughly 3-mile paddling loop around Horseshoe Lake. A dedicated boat launch dock near the park entrance makes access easy, and paddleboards, kayaks, and sailboards are all permitted. Mussel inspection is required before launching.

An ADA-accessible paddle craft dock on San Leandro Bay with a free two-lane boat launch off Doolittle Drive and open estuary access. Strong bird watching runs along the restored wetlands adjacent to the ramp. The Tidewater Boating Center on site offers rowing and kayak programs for those wanting instruction.

A scenic East Bay Regional Park reservoir with a designated cartop and paddleboard launch at $2 per vessel plus a $4 mussel inspection fee. The surrounding hills offer a quiet feel with minimal powerboat traffic. SUP and windsurf users must shower for at least two minutes before entering the water per Contra Costa Water District rules.

A Bay Water Trail site on the San Joaquin River near its confluence with the Sacramento River, featuring ADA kayak launch facilities and on-dock storage racks for SUPs and kayaks. Delta Kayak Adventures, based at the marina, offers SUP rentals, tours, and guided sunset paddles into the Delta. Tidal and wind conditions require intermediate skills, especially in summer afternoon westerlies.

A compact urban park on the Hayward-Castro Valley border with a swim lagoon where kayak and SUP rentals are available during the summer season. The lagoon is calm and great for absolute beginners or families. Parking is $5; the adjacent San Lorenzo Creek reservoir is closed to all watercraft.

A 24-hour public boat launch on Dutch Slough with dock ramps on both sides, providing direct access into the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta's backwater channels. The narrow sloughs off Dutch Slough toward Sand Mound and Franks Tract offer protected flatwater when winds are calm. The $10 launch fee covers all vessels regardless of size.
geocode_display: Viking Harbor / Dutch Slough Boat Launch, 2140 Dutch Slough Rd, Oakley, CA 94561

notes: A narrow, rocky beach tucked inside a 19-acre Marin County park on San Francisco Bay, with a dedicated kayak and canoe launch near the fishing pier. The crossing to Angel Island is about 2.5 miles and regularly brings paddlers alongside harbor seals. Entry fees were permanently eliminated by the Marin Board of Supervisors.

A sheltered tidal creek launch next to the Larkspur Landing ferry terminal, with rentals available on-site through Outback Adventures. Paddlers get about 2 miles of meandering slough to explore, shielded from bay winds and ideal for beginners. Tide awareness helps avoid shallow sections near the Bon Air Road turnaround.

A full-service SUP and kayak shop (101 Surf Sports) on the San Rafael Canal with its own dock, launching paddlers onto calm canal water that feeds into San Pablo Bay about a mile out. Rentals, lessons, and board storage are all available on-site. Wind can pick up on the open bay section, so mornings are generally calmer.

A brackish coastal lagoon inside Point Reyes National Seashore, reachable by carrying your board 1.1 miles from the Pierce Point Road trailhead. The calm, wildlife-rich water is spectacular for an off-the-beaten-path paddle among shorebirds and waterfowl, with the ocean visible just beyond the dunes. No dogs on the trail, and nesting restrictions apply March through September.

A Sonoma County regional park straddling the spit between the ocean and Bodega Harbor, with an ADA-accessible kayak launch and a full boat ramp for trailers. The protected harbor side is calm and perfect for SUP, while the outer bay offers a chance to spot harbor seals hauled out on rocky outcroppings. Parking and launch access require the standard $7 day-use fee.

California's largest natural freshwater lake offers miles of warm, flat paddling with a dedicated boat ramp inside the state park at 5300 Soda Bay Road. The sheltered coves near the park are calm and beginner-friendly, and water temperatures run noticeably warmer than Bay Area reservoirs. The $8 day-use fee covers park entry and launch access.

San Francisco's largest freshwater lake sits in the city's southwestern corner, ringed by a 4.5-mile paved trail and free of tides and currents. The boathouse managed jointly by SF Rec and Parks and SF State University at 1 Harding Boulevard offers paddleboard and kayak rentals. It's one of the calmest and most predictable paddling spots in the city.

A wide sandy beach inside the Presidio where paddlers launch directly onto San Francisco Bay with one of the most dramatic urban backdrops in the world. Rental and lesson operations run from the beach seasonally. Wind and tide-driven currents are common here, making it better suited to intermediate and experienced paddlers; morning sessions before the afternoon westerlies are the sweet spot.

California's first urban state park puts paddlers onto the southeastern corner of San Francisco Bay from Jackrabbit Beach, a sandy launch that requires higher tide for good access. The park draws windsurfers but also kayak and SUP paddlers looking for a less-crowded city put-in with open bay views. Dogs are allowed on leash throughout the park.

Where the American River meets the Sacramento River, Discovery Park offers a six-lane boat ramp and direct access to two wild and scenic rivers for SUPs and kayaks. The American River Parkway bike trail runs alongside, making a combined paddle-and-ride day easy. Parking fees apply year-round; dogs must be on leash.

Lake Natoma at Nimbus Flat is one of the premier flatwater SUP destinations in the Sacramento region, with a strict 5 mph motorized boat limit keeping conditions glassy and calm. The Sacramento State Aquatic Center on site rents kayaks and SUPs to the public. A $12 parking fee applies; no additional launch fee.

Granite Bay is the most popular beach access point on Folsom Lake, with four concrete launch ramps and ample sandy shore for hand-launching kayaks and SUPs. On-site concessions rent paddleboards, kayaks, and rafts seasonally. Parking is $12; hand-launched boats have no additional launch fee.

Surrounded by granite peaks and pine forest, Donner Lake offers crystal-clear alpine paddling just off I-80 near Truckee. The public ramp on the northwest shore and 37 first-come public docks make launching easy. Watercraft self-inspection is mandatory; power boats are allowed with a 35 mph speed limit.

The largest public sandy beach on Lake Tahoe's north shore, with 1,700 feet of lakefront and multiple SUP and kayak rental shops steps from the water. The Coon Street launch ramp serves non-motorized vessels only. Aquatic invasive species inspection is required before launching on Lake Tahoe.

Echo Lakes sits at 7,414 feet in the El Dorado National Forest, gateway to Desolation Wilderness, with deep blue water framed by glaciated granite. Echo Chalet charges $7 to launch kayaks and SUPs; the 4.5-mile round-trip across Lower and Upper Echo Lakes is a classic Sierra paddle. Invasive species inspection is required.

Set at 7,000 feet in the Sierra National Forest, Huntington Lake is a wind-swept alpine reservoir prized by sailors and paddlers alike. The public boat ramp between College and Deer Creek Campgrounds charges $3 to launch. Afternoon winds can pick up quickly, so an early morning start is recommended for SUP.

A sapphire gem at 5,370 feet elevation in the Sierra National Forest above Fresno, with the north-shore launch ramp and Camp Edison marina both offering easy water access. The marina rents SUPs, kayaks, and canoes. Afternoon winds can develop, making morning paddles the best bet.

A sprawling El Dorado National Forest lake reached via Ice House Road, with three free launch ramps at Sunset, Yellowjacket, and Westpoint campgrounds. The calm, forested shoreline makes for excellent beginner SUP and kayak touring. Water skiing and wake boats also use the lake, so keep close to the coves.

A vast Gold Country reservoir in the Stanislaus foothills with miles of quiet coves and arms ideal for SUP. The Glory Hole Recreation Area provides a boat launch with no day-use fee. Rental delivery services are available directly at the ramp for those without their own gear.

One of California's premier recreation reservoirs in the Sierra Nevada foothills, with paved launch ramps at Fleming Meadows and Moccasin Point and rental equipment available at the marina. The warm water and dramatic canyon scenery make it a worthy drive from the Bay Area. Mornings are calmest; afternoon wind is common across the main lake.

A warm-water reservoir in the Calaveras County foothills with four launch ramps and miles of quiet coves for paddling. The $5 day-use fee covers launching any number of boats with access to carry-down walkways and boarding floats. Verify current ramp conditions before visiting, as invasive mussel protocols occasionally close individual ramps.

A Stanislaus County regional park reservoir a few miles north of Oakdale, with kayaking and canoeing allowed within 200 feet of the shoreline. The Bayview Boat Ramp provides easy access, and a 5 mph speed zone covers most arms. Water contact is restricted from Labor Day through May, so plan visits during the warmer season.

Morro Bay's protected estuary is one of the Central Coast's top paddling destinations, with the Coleman Park small beach offering the easiest SUP and kayak entry at any tide level. Multiple rental shops along the Embarcadero provide boards and guided tours. Wildlife sightings including sea otters, harbor seals, and shorebirds are common throughout the bay.

Old Port Beach at Port San Luis offers a free, drive-to launch where at low tide you can roll gear straight to the waterline. The protected cove delivers calmer and warmer conditions than most Central Coast spots, with clear views from Point Sal to Point San Luis. Avila Beach Paddlesports operates rental and tour services directly on the harbor.

Carmel River State Beach includes a calm tidal lagoon and wetlands reserve, offering sheltered flatwater paddling through marshy channels thick with shorebirds just south of Carmel. Parking and launch access are free, with a short portage from the lot to the water's edge. Tidal conditions affect lagoon depth, and the ocean mouth area can be hazardous.

water: Willow Creek Recreation Area - Lake Natoma
notes: Willow Creek Recreation Area sits on the western end of Lake Natoma within Folsom Lake State Recreation Area, offering a calm, sheltered launch into a non-motorized lake. The paved ramp drops you into flat water that stays quiet and wind-sheltered, ideal for beginners and easy morning paddles. Day-use fee is $10 (Poppy Pass / Golden Poppy Annual Pass accepted). Restrooms and covered picnic tables on site. Access from westbound Folsom Blvd between Blue Ravine Rd and Natoma Station Drive.
geocode_display: Willow Creek Recreation Area, 13303 Folsom Blvd, Folsom, CA 95630
